questions.question: An IPv4 datagram must cross a link whose MTU is smaller than the datagram. Which field in the IPv4 header tells routers they must NOT fragment the packet and should instead drop it and send an error?
options: The Identification field set to 0xFFFF
The DF (Don't Fragment) bit in the Flags field
The MF (More Fragments) bit in the Flags field
The Fragment Offset field set to 8191
questions.explanation: The DF (Don't Fragment) bit is bit 1 of the 3-bit Flags field in the IPv4 header. When DF=1, a router that cannot forward the packet without fragmenting must drop it and send an ICMP Type 3 Code 4 message back to the source instead.

questions.question: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) has an MTU of 1500 bytes while IPv6 requires routers to forward packets of at least 1280 bytes. What is the PRIMARY reason different network technologies have different MTUs?
options: All technologies use the same MTU; differences are just misconfigurations
Causes vary: hardware DMA buffer sizes, protocol field widths, retransmission economics, and standards compliance all play a role
MTU is always negotiated dynamically via DHCP and varies per connection
MTU differences exist only between IPv4 and IPv6, not within the same protocol family
questions.explanation: MTU limits arise from multiple independent causes: hardware constraints (early NIC DMA buffers), OS kernel buffer granularity, protocol field widths (IPv4 Total Length is 16 bits, capping at 65,535 bytes), international standards like ATM cell sizes, and link economics around retransmission cost and channel fairness.

questions.question: An IPv4 datagram has a 20-byte header and 1400 bytes of data and must cross a link with MTU=620 bytes. After computing maximum aligned data per fragment = floor((620-20)/8)*8 = 600 bytes, which of the following correctly describes the THIRD fragment?
options: offset_field=150, data=200 bytes, MF=0, total_len=220
offset_field=75, data=600 bytes, MF=1, total_len=620
offset_field=150, data=200 bytes, MF=1, total_len=220
offset_field=1200, data=200 bytes, MF=0, total_len=220
questions.explanation: Fragment 1 carries bytes 0-599 (offset=0, field=0); Fragment 2 carries bytes 600-1199 (offset=600, field=75); Fragment 3 carries bytes 1200-1399 (200 bytes, offset_field=1200/8=150, MF=0 because it is the last fragment, total_len=20+200=220).

questions.question: IPv4 uses NONTRANSPARENT fragmentation. Which statement correctly describes what this means and its main trade-off compared to transparent fragmentation?
options: Each intermediate router fragments AND immediately reassembles; the trade-off is extra CPU work at every hop
Fragments are forwarded independently to the destination, which reassembles them; the trade-off is that loss of any single fragment requires retransmitting the entire original datagram
The source pre-fragments before sending; routers are unaware; the trade-off is higher source CPU usage
Fragmentation is transparent to the IP layer because it is handled entirely by TCP; the trade-off is that UDP cannot use it
questions.explanation: Nontransparent (end-to-end) IP fragmentation forwards fragments independently — they may even take different routes — and only the destination reassembles them. This keeps routers simple and allows multipath routing, but means any lost fragment forces retransmission of the entire original datagram, because IP offers no partial-delivery guarantee.

questions.question: During Path MTU Discovery (RFC 1191), a source sends a 1400-byte packet with DF=1. Router R1 (MTU=1200) drops it and sends ICMP Type 3 Code 4. The source retransmits at 1200 bytes. Router R2 (MTU=900) drops it and sends another ICMP. What does the source do next, and what is the final cached path MTU?
options: The source sets DF=0 and lets R2 fragment the 1200-byte packet
The source retransmits at 900 bytes; the cached path MTU is 900 bytes
The source abandons the connection because two ICMP errors indicate a black hole
The source retransmits at 1200 bytes with a different Identification value
questions.explanation: Path MTU Discovery is iterative: each ICMP 'fragmentation needed' tells the source the MTU of the blocking hop. The source reduces its packet size to that value and retransmits with DF=1 still set. After R2 reports MTU=900, the source retransmits at 900 bytes. If no further ICMP is received, 900 bytes is the path MTU cached for this destination.

questions.question: A firewall on the path blocks all ICMP messages. A source sends DF=1 packets that exceed the tunnel MTU. What failure mode occurs, and which RFC documents the workaround?
options: The router automatically sets DF=0 and fragments the packet; no failure occurs
The packets are silently dropped and the source never learns the path MTU — a 'PMTU black hole'; RFC 2923 documents detection and workarounds such as PMTUD black-hole detection with DF=0 retry
The destination sends a TCP RST because fragments arrive out of order
The source automatically discovers the path MTU using UDP probes defined in RFC 1981
questions.explanation: When ICMP Type 3 Code 4 messages are filtered, the source never receives feedback that its DF=1 packets are being dropped. The connection appears to hang — the classic 'PMTU black hole'. RFC 2923 (TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery) documents this failure and mitigations such as retrying with DF=0 after a timeout or using TCP MSS negotiation to avoid oversized segments.
